title: Lynch: what you know, and how fast it grows

diagnosticQuestions.prompt: The “local advantage” in Lynch’s style means:
choices: You can observe products and services in real life — clues before spreadsheets
You must only buy local pizza shops
P/E to expected growth — a rough sanity check on what you pay vs growth
diagnosticQuestions.hint: High P/E vs low growth can be a mismatch.

teachSlides.title: Six buckets
teachSlides.body: Lynch famously sorted companies: slow growers, stalwarts, fast growers, cyclicals, turnarounds, asset plays. Each needs different expectations and timelines.

teachSlides.title: GARP in one breath
teachSlides.body: Growth at a reasonable price: you want growth, but you don’t want to pay the sky if growth is modest.

teachSlides.title: Stories change
teachSlides.body: A fast grower can become a stalwart. A turnaround can fail. Categories help you ask the right question at the right time.

recapSlide.title: Recap
recapSlide.body: Use what you observe, classify the company, and ask whether the price fits the growth story. PEG is a rough guide, not scripture.

masteryQuestions.prompt: A company has P/E 20 and long-term earnings growth near 5%. Many GARP-minded investors would:
choices: Question whether the price fits the growth — P/E above growth can be a red flag
masteryQuestions.explanation: Paying a high multiple for slow growth is often uncomfortable in Lynch-style thinking.

masteryQuestions.prompt: A cyclical (e.g. autos, chemicals at peak earnings) often:
choices: Needs a different lens than a fast grower — cycles mean highs can precede pain
masteryQuestions.explanation: Buying cyclicals near peak perception is a classic mistake.

choices: Large, steady firms with moderate growth and often dividends
masteryQuestions.explanation: They’re the middle path: not hyper-growth, not pure speculation.
